name: Esagila
description: Esagila, meaning 'House whose head is lofty,' was a temple dedicated to Marduk, the chief god of Babylon, located in the heart of the city. It stood as the most important religious structure in Babylonia, symbolizing the power and legitimacy of Babylonian kings. The temple complex housed numerous shrines and served as the center of Babylonian religious life, festivals, and rituals. Esagila was not merely a place of worship but a cosmic axis, believed to be the earthly counterpart of Marduk's celestial abode. Its construction and maintenance were considered essential for maintaining cosmic order and ensuring the prosperity of Babylon. The temple's destruction and restoration reflected the rise and fall of empires, highlighting its profound connection to the city's destiny. Royal inscriptions frequently mention Esagila, detailing its rebuilding and embellishment by various rulers, underscoring its enduring significance throughout Babylonian history. The ziggurat Etemenanki, often associated with the Tower of Babel, was a prominent part of the Esagila complex.
subtitle: House of Marduk, Center of Babylon

keyMyths.title: Marduk's Ascension
keyMyths.description: Esagila was the location where Marduk was worshipped as the supreme god after his victory over Tiamat. The Enuma Elish describes Marduk's enthronement and the establishment of his cult in Esagila.

keyMyths.title: Royal Inauguration
keyMyths.description: Babylonian kings were traditionally inaugurated at Esagila, receiving their authority and legitimacy from Marduk. The king would participate in rituals within the temple, solidifying his role as Marduk's representative on Earth.

sources.author: Stephanie Dalley
sources.description: A collection of translated Mesopotamian myths and legends.
sources.text: A collection of translated Mesopotamian myths and legends.

sources.author: Gwendolyn Leick
